threats.scenario: Product search endpoint GET /rest/products/search uses raw SQL: SELECT * FROM Products WHERE ((name LIKE '%${criteria}%' ... (routes/search.ts:21). Attacker can inject UNION SELECT to extract all database content including user emails and MD5 password hashes.

remediation.title: Replace raw SQL in product search with Sequelize ORM query
steps: Replace sequelize.query with ProductModel.findAll({ where: { [Op.or]: [{ name: { [Op.like]: '%' + criteria + '%' } }, { description: { [Op.like]: '%' + criteria + '%' } }] } })
Never concatenate user input into SQL strings

threats.scenario: B2B Order endpoint POST /b2b/v2/orders evaluates user-controlled orderLinesData via vm.runInContext('safeEval(orderLinesData)', sandbox) in routes/b2bOrder.ts:22. Even with notevil sandbox, vm.createContext escapes are known. Attacker can achieve Remote Code Execution on the Node.js server.

remediation.title: Remove eval/vm.runInContext from B2B order processing
steps: Remove the vm.runInContext and safeEval calls entirely
Parse orderLinesData as JSON with a strict schema validator (e.g. Zod or Joi)
Never evaluate user-controlled code on the server side

threats.scenario: PATCH /rest/products/reviews uses NoSQL injection via { _id: req.body.id } with { multi: true } (routes/updateProductReviews.ts:18). Attacker can send a MongoDB operator like { '$gt': '' } as id to match all documents and overwrite all product reviews simultaneously.

remediation.title: Sanitize NoSQL query inputs; remove multi:true for user-controlled updates
steps: Validate that id is a string, not an object, before using in query
Remove { multi: true } option — each user should only update their own review
Use Object.prototype.toString.call(id) === '[object String]' check

threats.scenario: Order tracking GET /rest/track-order/:id uses $where JavaScript operator: db.ordersCollection.find({ $where: `this.orderId === '${id}'` }) (routes/trackOrder.ts:18). Attacker can inject JavaScript to exfiltrate all orders, access orders of other users (IDOR), or perform timing attacks.
threats.controls_in_place: Regex strip on non-challenge path (insufficient, challenge path unprotected)

remediation.title: Replace $where NoSQL injection with direct field comparison
steps: Replace $where with { orderId: id }
Sanitize id to alphanumeric characters only

threats.scenario: CORS is fully open: app.options('*', cors()); app.use(cors()); (server.ts:181-182). No origin allowlist. Any malicious website can make credentialed cross-origin requests to all API endpoints, including authenticated ones, from a victim's browser.

remediation.title: Configure CORS with explicit origin allowlist
steps: Replace app.use(cors()) with app.use(cors({ origin: ['https://your-frontend.example.com'], credentials: true }))
Never use wildcard with credentials

threats.scenario: errorhandler() middleware (server.ts:676) sends full stack traces in HTTP responses in non-production environments. Application version, file paths, and internal implementation details are disclosed to attackers via error responses.

remediation.title: Remove errorhandler() middleware; implement custom safe error handler
steps: Remove errorhandler() import and usage
Add custom error handler: app.use((err, req, res, next) => { logger.error(err); res.status(500).json({ error: 'Internal server error' }) })

threats.scenario: FTP directory is publicly browsable at /ftp with serve-index (server.ts:255). Files include: incident-support.kdbx (KeePass database), coupons_2013.md.bak (credential hints), package-lock.json.bak (dependency inventory). Encryption keys at /encryptionkeys/ also browsable.

remediation.title: Remove public FTP directory listing and encryption key directory exposure
steps: Remove serve-index for /ftp endpoint
Move sensitive files out of publicly accessible directories
Require authentication for any file download endpoints

threats.scenario: SSRF via profile image URL upload: POST /profile/image/url fetches arbitrary URLs provided by the user (routes/profileImageUrlUpload.ts:26) without allowlist validation. Attacker can make the server fetch internal services (metadata endpoints, localhost services, internal APIs).
threats.controls_in_place: Only SSRF challenge detection — no actual blocking

remediation.title: Add URL allowlist or DNS resolution check before fetching profile image URL
steps: Validate URL scheme is https only
Validate host against allowlist of image CDNs
Block requests to RFC 1918 addresses and localhost

threats.scenario: Unauthenticated Prometheus metrics endpoint GET /metrics (server.ts:718) exposes internal operational data: request counts, error rates, response times, file upload statistics. This reveals the technology stack, internal URL patterns, and operational state to attackers.

remediation.title: Protect /metrics endpoint with authentication or restrict to internal network
steps: Add IP allowlist or bearer token requirement to /metrics route
Alternatively, serve metrics on an internal port not exposed externally

threats.scenario: Open redirect at GET /redirect?to= (routes/redirect.ts:15). isRedirectAllowed() checks against a URL prefix allowlist, but bypass techniques exist (URL encoding, redirects through allowed domains). Attacker can craft phishing links that start with juice-shop domain but redirect to attacker site.

remediation.title: Replace open redirect with redirect code map; remove user-controlled URLs
steps: Replace to= URL parameter with a redirect code (e.g. to=checkout)
Maintain server-side mapping from codes to URLs
Never redirect to user-supplied full URLs

threats.scenario: GET /rest/admin/application-configuration returns the full application configuration object (routes/appConfiguration.ts:10). No authentication required. Response includes internal configuration parameters, Google OAuth client IDs, security-relevant settings, and server base URLs.

remediation.title: Require authentication for admin configuration endpoint; limit response fields
steps: Add security.isAuthorized() middleware before retrieveAppConfiguration()
Filter the config response to exclude sensitive fields
Require admin role for full configuration access

threats.scenario: No Content-Security-Policy headers (server.ts — CSP not configured). No CSRF protection (no csurf, no SameSite cookie attribute). Attacker can host a malicious page that submits state-changing forms/XHR requests to Juice Shop API using a logged-in user's cookies, triggering purchases, password changes, data deletion.

remediation.title: Add CSRF protection and Content-Security-Policy headers
steps: Implement SameSite=Lax on session cookies
Add helmet.contentSecurityPolicy() with a restrictive policy
Set Strict-Transport-Security header via helmet.hsts()
